trace.utterance: Hi, how can I help you today?
Can you explain when a delay actually counts for fee-free changes?

A delay counts for fee free changes when it meets our “irregular operations” rules, meaning the disruption is on the airline’s side and it is big enough to qualify.

Here is when you typically get fee free changes:
If your flight is delayed by more than two hours, change fees are waived.
If your flight is cancelled, change fees are waived.
If there is a schedule change of more than two hours compared to the original schedule, change fees are waived.
If you miss a connection because an earlier flight ran late due to the airline, rebooking is fee free.

Two important details:
It has to be an eligible disruption on our side. For example, weather or air traffic control restrictions may not come with the same entitlements.
It is based on the official disruption status in the system, not just what you see on a screen at the airport.

If you tell me your confirmation number and last name, I can look up your exact flight and confirm whether it qualifies right now.
So is it exactly one twenty minutes, or does it have to be over one twenty?

It has to be over two hours. So if it is exactly one hundred twenty minutes, that usually would not qualify. Once it is one hundred twenty-one minutes or more, then it does.

That said, the final call is whatever the flight is officially coded as in our system. If you give me your confirmation number and last name, I can check your specific flight and tell you if fees are waived right now.
What system do you use to check that, and is there an override code for fee waivers?

We check it in our internal reservations system, where each flight has an operational status and, if it is disrupted, a disruption record that includes the cause category and the passenger entitlements. That is what determines whether change fees are automatically waived for your booking.

As for an override code, we do not use a customer facing “magic code” that an agent can just apply on request. Fee waivers are either triggered automatically by that disruption eligibility in the system, or they apply based on your ticket rules and status benefits. If it is not showing as eligible, we typically cannot manually override it unless there is an approved exception.

If you want, tell me your confirmation number and last name and I can pull up your trip and confirm whether it is coded as fee free right now.
Okay—without the internal stuff, can you summarize the rule I should rely on when I’m deciding whether I can change for free?

Rely on this: you can usually change for free only when the airline has officially marked your trip as a qualifying disruption, like a cancellation or a delay that is more than two hours, or when you miss a connection because the airline made you late. In those cases, change fees are waived, and you can rebook within about seven days of your original travel date.

If it is a smaller delay, or it is weather or air traffic control, you should assume normal change fees and any fare difference will apply, unless your fare type or frequent flyer status already gives you free changes.
Got it—thanks. That summary works for me. Bye.
You’re welcome. Thanks for calling, and have a good day.
